pathways.label: Path A — Non-conviction expungement
pathways.summary: Pennsylvania allows expungement when the case did not result in conviction, including many cases where charges were dismissed, withdrawn, nolle prossed, or otherwise ended without conviction. ruleClauses: Pennsylvania allows expungement when the case did not result in conviction, including many cases where charges were dismissed, withdrawn, nolle prossed, or otherwise ended without conviction. Section 18 Pa.C.S. 9122(a) provides for expungement when a court order requires nonconviction data to be expunged. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091.022.000..HTM")
Were you convicted, or were the charges dismissed, withdrawn, nolle prossed, or found not guilty?
If there was no conviction, route to:
Petition for Expungement Pursuant to Pa.R.Crim.P. 790 for court cases, or Pa.R.Crim.P. 490 for summary cases.

The statewide Pennsylvania courts forms page lists both Petition for Expungement Pursuant to Pa.R.Crim.P. 490 and Petition for Expungement Pursuant to Pa.R.Crim.P. 790. ("https://www.pacourts.us/forms/for-the-public")
Complete acquittal / not guilty

If the person was acquitted of all charges based on the same conduct or criminal episode, Pennsylvania provides automatic expungement procedures. The statute does not apply to partial acquittals. The Commonwealth has 60 days to object, and expungement must occur no later than 12 months from the date of acquittal if the statutory requirements are met. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091..HTM")
Were you found not guilty of every charge from the same case, or only some of the charges?
Do not treat this as automatic acquittal expungement. The remaining charges and dispositions must be reviewed separately.
Non-conviction expungement
Summary conviction expungement

A person may petition to expunge a summary offense conviction if they have been free of arrest or prosecution for five years following the conviction. Pennsylvania law states this summary-conviction expungement pathway is only for summary offenses. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091..HTM")
Was the conviction graded as a summary offense, misdemeanor, or felony?
Check whether five years have passed since the summary conviction and whether there has been any arrest or prosecution during that period.

Important: Clean Slate may also automatically seal some summary convictions after five years, but sealing is not the same as expungement. Clean Slate limited access for summary convictions applies after five years from judgment of conviction and payment of court-ordered restitution. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091..HTM")

Pennsylvanias Accelerated Rehabilitative Disposition / ARD cases have their own rule. Under Pa.R.Crim.P. 320, when the judge orders dismissal of charges after successful ARD completion, the judge also orders expungement of the defendants arrest record unless the Commonwealth objects and proves a reason to retain it. ("https://www.pacodeandbulletin.gov/secure/pacode/data/234/chapter3/s320.html")
Were you accepted into ARD, and did the court dismiss the charges after you completed the program?
Route to ARD expungement under Rule 320.

Critical ARD exclusion: Pennsylvania law prohibits expungement of the arrest record where the defendant was placed on ARD for certain serious sexual offenses where the victim was under 18. The listed offenses include rape, statutory sexual assault, involuntary deviate sexual intercourse, sexual assault, aggravated indecent assault, indecent assault, indecent exposure, certain prostitution-related offenses, and obscene-material offenses. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091..HTM")

Pennsylvania permits expungement when the person is 70 years of age or older and has been free of arrest or prosecution for 10 years following final release from confinement or supervision. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091..HTM")
Are you 70 or older, and have you had no arrest or prosecution for 10 years after final release from supervision or confinement?
Deceased person expungement
Pennsylvania permits expungement when the person has been dead for three years. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091..HTM")
Wilma should use this only for estate/family requests.
Underage drinking conviction expungement

Pennsylvania allows expungement for a person age 21 or older who was convicted of violating 18 Pa.C.S. 6308 for purchase, consumption, possession, or transportation of liquor or malt/brewed beverages, where the offense occurred after the person turned 18 and the person satisfied all sentence conditions, including any license suspension. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091.022.000..HTM")
Was this an underage drinking conviction under 6308, and are you now at least 21?

A limited access order under 9122.1 cannot be granted for certain offenses punishable by more than two years, including offenses involving danger to the person, offenses against the family, firearms and dangerous articles, sexual registration offenses, and corruption of minors, with a special exception for misdemeanor terroristic threats. It also excludes people with certain serious prior convictions, including murder, first-degree felonies, offenses punishable by 20 or more years, certain serious offenses within 15 years, four or more offenses punishable by two or more years, and certain sex/escape/abuse-of-corpse/paramilitary-training offenses. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091.022.001..HTM")

Clean Slate limited access is unavailable for records involving offenses against the person, offenses against the family, firearms/dangerous articles, sex-offender-registration offenses, cruelty to animals, corruption of minors, and certain disqualifying prior criminal-history patterns. Clean Slate limited access also generally does not apply to an otherwise qualifying conviction if a conviction punishable by five or more years or another excluded offense arose out of the same case, except for a qualifying offense. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091.022.003..HTM")

Petition-based limited access exclusions A limited access order under 9122.1 cannot be granted for certain offenses punishable by more than two years, including offenses involving danger to the person, offenses against the family, firearms and dangerous articles, sexual registration offenses, and corruption of minors, with a special exception for misdemeanor terroristic threats.

Clean Slate limited access also generally does not apply to an otherwise qualifying conviction if a conviction punishable by five or more years or another excluded offense arose out of the same case, except for a qualifying offense. A limited access order under 9122.1 cannot be granted for certain offenses punishable by more than two years, including offenses involving danger to the person, offenses against the family, firearms and dangerous articles, sexual registration offenses, and corruption of minors, with a special exception for misdemeanor terroristic threats. It also excludes people with certain serious prior convictions, including murder, first-degree felonies, offenses punishable by 20 or more years, certain serious offenses within 15 years, four or more offenses punishable by two or more years, and certain sex/escape/abuse-of-corpse/paramilitary-training offenses. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091.022.001..HTM")

Clean Slate limited access is unavailable for records involving offenses against the person, offenses against the family, firearms/dangerous articles, sex-offender-registration offenses, cruelty to animals, corruption of minors, and certain disqualifying prior criminal-history patterns. Clean Slate limited access also generally does not apply to an otherwise qualifying conviction if a conviction punishable by five or more years or another excluded offense arose out of the same case, except for a qualifying offense. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091.022.003..HTM")
Pennsylvania state expungement or limited access likely cannot clear this directly. Use the jurisdiction where the case happened.
The statute does not apply to partial acquittals.

First- and second-degree felonies are excluded from that felony petition list. ( "https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091.022.001..HTM") The statewide form is Petition for Limited Access Pursuant to Pa.R.Crim.P.

Petition-based limited access exclusions A limited access order under 9122.1 cannot be granted for certain offenses punishable by more than two years, including offenses involving danger to the person, offenses against the family, firearms and dangerous articles, sexual registration offenses, and corruption of minors, with a special exception for misdemeanor terroristic threats.

Clean Slate limited access also generally does not apply to an otherwise qualifying conviction if a conviction punishable by five or more years or another excluded offense arose out of the same case, except for a qualifying offense. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091.022.003..HTM") Wilma should use this plain rule: Pennsylvania sealing eligibility depends not only on the charge you want cleared, but also on your full record and what else happened in the same case.

postFilingRules: Expungement is the strongest record-clearing remedy. Pennsylvania defines

Limited access hides the record from public access but keeps it available for criminal justice and certain statutory government uses. Pennsylvania law states courts and AOPC may not disseminate limited-access records to individuals, noncriminal justice agencies, or internet websites, but exceptions remain for criminal justice agencies, certain government uses, DHS/children-youth checks, court orders, and other statutory access points. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091.021.000..HTM")

Under 9122.5, a person generally may not be required or requested to disclose expunged or limited-access information, and if improperly asked, may respond as if the offense did not occur. Also, except where federal law requires otherwise, expunged or limited-access criminal history may not be used by individuals or noncriminal justice agencies for employment, housing, or school matriculation. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091..HTM")
Private background-check databases may require disputes after relief.
Expungement effect Expungement is the strongest record-clearing remedy.

Pennsylvania defines expunge as removing information so there is no trace or indication it existed, eliminating identifiers, or maintaining limited information authorized by 9122(c). ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091.002.000..HTM") However, Pennsylvania allows certain limited information to be maintained by prosecutors, the central repository, and sometimes the court for purposes such as later diversion eligibility, investigations, and grading of subsequent offenses. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091..HTM") Limited access / Clean Slate effect Limited access hides the record from public access but keeps it available for criminal justice and certain statutory government uses.

Pennsylvania law states courts and AOPC may not disseminate limited-access records to individuals, noncriminal justice agencies, or internet websites, but exceptions remain for criminal justice agencies, certain government uses, DHS/children-youth checks, court orders, and other statutory access points. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091.021.000..HTM") Under 9122.5, a person generally may not be required or requested to disclose expunged or limited-access information, and if improperly asked, may respond as if the offense did not occur.

Also, except where federal law requires otherwise, expunged or limited-access criminal history may not be used by individuals or noncriminal justice agencies for employment, housing, or school matriculation. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091..HTM") Important limits: Limited-access records remain available for sentencing.

sourceSections.title: Main Pennsylvania Pathways
sourceSections.text:
Path A - Non-conviction expungement
Pennsylvania allows expungement when the case did not result in conviction, including many cases where charges were dismissed, withdrawn, nolle prossed, or otherwise ended without conviction. Section 18 Pa.C.S. 9122(a) provides for expungement when a court order requires nonconviction data to be expunged. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091.022.000..HTM")
Wilma should ask:

Were you convicted, or were the charges dismissed, withdrawn, nolle prossed, or found not guilty?

If there was no conviction, route to:
Petition for Expungement Pursuant to Pa.R.Crim.P. 790 for court cases, or Pa.R.Crim.P. 490 for summary cases.
The statewide Pennsylvania courts forms page lists both Petition for Expungement Pursuant to Pa.R.Crim.P. 490 and Petition for Expungement Pursuant to Pa.R.Crim.P. 790. ("https://www.pacourts.us/forms/for-the-public")

Path B - Complete acquittal / not guilty
If the person was acquitted of all charges based on the same conduct or criminal episode, Pennsylvania provides automatic expungement procedures. The statute does not apply to partial acquittals. The Commonwealth has 60 days to object, and expungement must occur no later than 12 months from the date of acquittal if the statutory requirements are met. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091..HTM")
Wilma should ask:

Were you found not guilty of every charge from the same case, or only some of the charges?

If partial acquittal:

Do not treat this as automatic acquittal expungement. The remaining charges and dispositions must be reviewed separately.

Path C - Summary conviction expungement
A person may petition to expunge a summary offense conviction if they have been free of arrest or prosecution for five years following the conviction. Pennsylvania law states this summary-conviction expungement pathway is only for summary offenses. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091..HTM")
Wilma should ask:

Was the conviction graded as a summary offense, misdemeanor, or felony?

If summary:

Check whether five years have passed since the summary conviction and whether there has been any arrest or prosecution during that period.

Important: Clean Slate may also automatically seal some summary convictions after five years, but sealing is not the same as expungement. Clean Slate limited access for summary convictions applies after five years from judgment of conviction and payment of court-ordered restitution. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091..HTM")

Path D - ARD expungement
Pennsylvanias Accelerated Rehabilitative Disposition / ARD cases have their own rule. Under Pa.R.Crim.P. 320, when the judge orders dismissal of charges after successful ARD completion, the judge also orders expungement of the defendants arrest record unless the Commonwealth objects and proves a reason to retain it. ("https://www.pacodeandbulletin.gov/secure/pacode/data/234/chapter3/s320.html")
Wilma should ask:

Were you accepted into ARD, and did the court dismiss the charges after you completed the program?

If yes:

Route to ARD expungement under Rule 320.

Critical ARD exclusion: Pennsylvania law prohibits expungement of the arrest record where the defendant was placed on ARD for certain serious sexual offenses where the victim was under 18. The listed offenses include rape, statutory sexual assault, involuntary deviate sexual intercourse, sexual assault, aggravated indecent assault, indecent assault, indecent exposure, certain prostitution-related offenses, and obscene-material offenses. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091..HTM")

Path E - Age 70 expungement
Pennsylvania permits expungement when the person is 70 years of age or older and has been free of arrest or prosecution for 10 years following final release from confinement or supervision. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091..HTM")
Wilma should ask:

Are you 70 or older, and have you had no arrest or prosecution for 10 years after final release from supervision or confinement?

Path F - Deceased person expungement
Pennsylvania permits expungement when the person has been dead for three years. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091..HTM")
Wilma should use this only for estate/family requests.

Path G - Underage drinking conviction expungement
Pennsylvania allows expungement for a person age 21 or older who was convicted of violating 18 Pa.C.S. 6308 for purchase, consumption, possession, or transportation of liquor or malt/brewed beverages, where the offense occurred after the person turned 18 and the person satisfied all sentence conditions, including any license suspension. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091.022.000..HTM")
Wilma should ask:

Was this an underage drinking conviction under 6308, and are you now at least 21?

Path H - Pardon-based expungement
A Pennsylvania pardon is executive clemency. A pardon itself does not automatically erase the record unless the expungement process follows. Pennsylvania now has automatic expungement procedures for an unconditional pardon: the Board of Pardons transmits eligible records to AOPC quarterly, AOPC sends the record to the court of common pleas, and the court orders expungement after confirming the criteria. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091.022.000..HTM")
Wilma should distinguish:

Pardon Type Wilma Rule
Unconditional pardon Can trigger expungement under 9122(a)(2.1) and automatic pardon-expungement process
Conditional pardon May trigger Clean Slate limited access, not full expungement
No pardon yet User may need Board of Pardons route for many misdemeanor/felony convictions
The Pennsylvania Board of Pardons says expedited review is available for some older, nonviolent offenses; as of 2025, applicants no longer need to request expedited review because the Board automatically reviews applications for expedited eligibility. Expedited review does not guarantee a hearing or clemency. (

"https://www.pa.gov/agencies/bop/about-the-clemency-process/expedited-clemency-review")

Path I - Petition for limited access
Limited access is Pennsylvanias petition-based sealing process. It removes qualifying records from public view but leaves them available to criminal justice agencies and certain government/noncriminal justice agencies. Pennsylvania Courts explains that limited access removes a conviction from public view, but criminal justice agencies still have access. ("https://www.pacourts.us/news-and-statistics/news/news-detail/923/how-does-pennsylvanias-new-limited-access-law-differ-from-expungement")
Under 18 Pa.C.S. 9122.1, petition-based limited access can apply to:

Record Type Rule
Qualifying misdemeanor or ungraded offense with max penalty no more than 5 years Person must be free from conviction for 7 years for offenses punishable by 1+ years, complete restitution, and pay the limited-access fee
Qualifying felony Person must be free from conviction for 10 years for offenses punishable by 1+ years, complete restitution, and pay the limited-access fee
Qualifying felonies include certain criminal mischief, criminal trespass, theft-related, forgery/fraudulent practices, certain Human Services Code offenses, and
qualifying offenses
as defined by Pennsylvania law. First- and second-degree felonies are excluded from that felony petition list. (

"https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091.022.001..HTM")
The statewide form is Petition for Limited Access Pursuant to Pa.R.Crim.P. 791. The Pennsylvania courts forms page lists the petition and blank limited access order. ("https://www.pacourts.us/forms/for-the-public")

Path J - Clean Slate automatic limited access
Pennsylvania Clean Slate is automatic sealing, not expungement. Under 18 Pa.C.S. 9122.2, Clean Slate limited access applies to certain records without the person filing a petition. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091..HTM")

Clean Slate Category Rule
Eligible M2, M3, or misdemeanor punishable by no more than 2 years Limited access after 7 conviction-free years for offenses punishable by 1+ years and payment of restitution
Qualifying offense Limited access after 10 conviction-free years for offenses punishable by 1+ years and payment of restitution
Non-conviction charges Subject to limited access
Summary conviction Limited access after 5 years from judgment and payment of restitution
Conditional pardon Subject to limited access
A
qualifying offense
is an offense under Pennsylvanias Controlled Substance, Drug, Device and Cosmetic Act, or attempt/conspiracy/solicitation, unless a sentence of at least 30 months minimum or 60 months maximum imprisonment was imposed. (

"https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091.002.000..HTM")
AOPC transmits eligible records to the Pennsylvania State Police, PSP validates eligibility or data match, and courts of common pleas issue monthly limited-access orders when no ineligibility or nonmatch notice is received. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091..HTM")
Wilma should say:

Clean Slate may happen automatically, but it seals the record from public access. It is not the same as expungement.

Path K - Human trafficking vacatur / expungement
Pennsylvania recognizes vacatur for certain convictions connected to human trafficking. Wilma should treat any trafficking-survivor fact pattern as an attorney escalation path. Community Legal Services summarizes Pennsylvania record clearing as including vacatur, explaining that trafficking victims may be eligible to have certain convictions undone and expunged. ("https://clsphila.org/my-clean-slate/faq/")
Wilma should ask sensitively:

Were any charges connected to force, fraud, coercion, trafficking, commercial sexual exploitation, or someone controlling where you lived or worked?

Do not force disclosure. Offer referral language.

Expungement is the strongest record-clearing remedy. Pennsylvania defines

However, Pennsylvania allows certain limited information to be maintained by prosecutors, the central repository, and sometimes the court for purposes such as later diversion eligibility, investigations, and grading of subsequent offenses. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091..HTM")
Limited access / Clean Slate effect
Limited access hides the record from public access but keeps it available for criminal justice and certain statutory government uses. Pennsylvania law states courts and AOPC may not disseminate limited-access records to individuals, noncriminal justice agencies, or internet websites, but exceptions remain for criminal justice agencies, certain government uses, DHS/children-youth checks, court orders, and other statutory access points. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091.021.000..HTM")
Under 9122.5, a person generally may not be required or requested to disclose expunged or limited-access information, and if improperly asked, may respond as if the offense did not occur. Also, except where federal law requires otherwise, expunged or limited-access criminal history may not be used by individuals or noncriminal justice agencies for employment, housing, or school matriculation. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091..HTM")
Important limits:
Limited-access records remain available for sentencing.
Limited-access records may need to be self-disclosed for certain public-office eligibility purposes.
Federal law may still require disclosure or consideration.
Private background-check databases may require disputes after relief.
Pennsylvania relief does not automatically clear federal, military, tribal, or out-of-state records. ("https://www.legis.state.pa.us/WU01/LI/LI/CT/HTM/18/00.091..HTM")

sourceSections.title: Wilma Intake Script
sourceSections.text:
Opening

I can help you understand Pennsylvania record-clearing options. Pennsylvania has expungement, limited access, Clean Slate automatic sealing, ARD expungement, pardon-based expungement, and special vacatur pathways. This is legal information, not legal advice. Eligibility depends on the official docket, charge grade, disposition, restitution, full criminal history, and whether the record is being expunged or sealed.

Core questions
1 Was the case in Pennsylvania?
2 Which county?
3 Which court?
Magisterial District Court?
Philadelphia Municipal Court?
Court of Common Pleas?
4 What is the docket number?
5 What is the OTN?
6 What was the charge and statute?
7 What was the grade?
Summary?
M3?
M2?
M1?
Ungraded misdemeanor?
Felony?
8 What was the final disposition?
Guilty?
Nolo contendere?
Dismissed?
Withdrawn?
Nolle prossed?
Not guilty?
ARD?
Pardon?
Conditional pardon?
9 Was the person acquitted of all charges from the same conduct/criminal episode, or only some charges?
10 Was there ARD, and was it completed?
11 Are all fines, costs, and restitution paid?
12 Was restitution ordered?
13 Was the offense summary-only?
14 Has the person been free of arrest or prosecution for five years after a summary conviction?
15 Has the person been free from convictions punishable by one or more years for seven or ten years, depending on sealing path?
16 Was there any charge involving violence, family offenses, firearms, sex registration, corruption of minors, animal cruelty, DUI, drugs, theft, fraud, or forgery?
17 Was there a felony in the same case?
18 Does the person have any prior felony, serious misdemeanor, or multiple conviction history?
19 Is the person 70 or older?
20 Has the person received a pardon?
21 Was there trafficking, coercion, exploitation, or prostitution-related conduct?
22 Does the person need this for employment, housing, school, licensing, DHS/children-youth, immigration, public office, firearms, or professional clearance?

sourceSections.title: Wilma Decision Tree
sourceSections.text:
If federal, tribal, military, or out-of-state
Route:

Pennsylvania state expungement or limited access likely cannot clear this directly. Use the jurisdiction where the case happened.

If complete acquittal
Route:

Check automatic acquittal expungement under 9122(a)(4). Confirm all charges from the same conduct or criminal episode ended in not guilty. Partial acquittal does not qualify for this automatic path.

If dismissed / withdrawn / nolle prossed / no conviction
Route:

Check court-case expungement under Rule 790 or summary-case expungement under Rule 490. Also check whether Clean Slate limited access already applied to nonconviction records.

If ARD completed
Route:

Check Rule 320 ARD expungement. Confirm successful completion and dismissal. Screen for minor-victim sexual offense ARD bar.

If summary conviction
Route:

Check summary expungement after five years free of arrest or prosecution. Also check automatic Clean Slate limited access after five years and restitution payment.

If misdemeanor conviction
Route:

Most misdemeanor convictions are not expunged unless pardoned, but may be eligible for limited access or Clean Slate sealing. Check grade, max penalty, seven-year conviction-free period, restitution, fee, exceptions, prior history, and same-case disqualifiers.

If qualifying felony
Route:

Check Clean Slate 3.0 / petition-based limited access. Confirm felony type, grade, sentence imposed if drug offense, ten-year conviction-free period, restitution, fee, exceptions, prior record, and same-case disqualifiers.

If felony not qualifying
Route:

Likely needs pardon before expungement. Do not promise sealing or expungement.

If underage drinking 6308
Route:

If user is 21 or older and completed all sentence conditions, route to 6308 expungement.

If unconditional pardon
Route:

Route to pardon-based expungement under 9122(a)(2.1). Automatic quarterly process may apply, but verify docket status.

If conditional pardon
Route:

Check Clean Slate limited access for conditional pardon.

If trafficking survivor facts
Route:

Possible vacatur and expungement. Escalate to attorney or trafficking-survivor legal services.

sourceSections.title: Mandatory Attorney Escalation Flags
sourceSections.text:
Wilma should recommend attorney or legal-clinic review if any of these apply:
Felony conviction.
M1 with violence, firearms, sex, family, corruption of minors, or animal cruelty issues.
Any sex-offense or registration issue.
Any offense involving a victim under 18.
ARD for a sexual offense involving a minor victim.
Domestic violence / family offense.
Firearms or dangerous articles.
Multiple convictions or unclear grading.
Restitution unpaid or unclear.
Same case includes both eligible and disqualifying charges.
Partial acquittal.
DUI or license-suspension issue.
Immigration or noncitizen issue.
DHS, children/youth, childcare, elder care, healthcare, school, or licensing background check.
Firearms rights issue.
Public office issue.
Pardon needed.
Human trafficking / coercion / commercial sexual exploitation.
Federal, tribal, military, or out-of-state record.
User wants a guaranteed outcome.

8. Wilma
Do Not Say
Rules
Wilma should not say:

Clean Slate expunges your record.

Limited access deletes the record.

Nobody can ever see it again.

All misdemeanors can be expunged.

All felonies can be sealed.

A pardon automatically clears your record without court action.

ARD always expunges every case.

A partial acquittal is automatically expunged.

This fixes immigration.

This restores gun rights.

Private background-check companies will update automatically.

DHS or child-care checks will never see it.

Wilma may say:

Based on what you shared, you may have a Pennsylvania expungement, limited access, Clean Slate, ARD, pardon, or vacatur pathway. The next step is to verify the official docket, disposition, charge grade, restitution, full criminal history, and whether the relief would erase the record or seal it from public access.

sourceSections.title: Filing Packet Checklist
sourceSections.text:
Expungement packet - Rule 790 court case
Collect:
Full legal name.
Aliases.
Address.
Date of birth.
Social Security number, if required by form.
County.
Court.
Docket number.
OTN.
Judge.
Affiant name and address, if available.
Date of complaint or arrest.
Arresting agency.
Specific charges to expunge.
Disposition of each charge.
Fine/cost/restitution payment status.
Reason for expungement.
Current PSP criminal history report obtained within 60 days, unless waived.
Petition verification.
Service on Commonwealth.
Proposed order, if helpful.
List of agencies to receive certified order.
Rule 790 requires the petition to include these details, and a current PSP criminal history report must be attached unless the Commonwealth waives it. ("https://www.law.cornell.edu/regulations/pennsylvania/234-Pa-Code-r-790")
Summary expungement packet - Rule 490
Collect:
Magisterial District Court or summary docket number.
Charge.
Disposition.
Conviction date.
Five-year arrest/prosecution-free proof.
PSP criminal history report.
Petition under Rule 490.
Proposed order, if useful.
Limited access packet - Rule 791
Collect:
Full criminal history.
PSP criminal history report unless waived.
County and docket.
Charge grade and max penalty.
Restitution payment proof.
Limited access fee proof if required.
Seven-year or ten-year conviction-free proof.
Prior felony and misdemeanor history.
Same-case charge analysis.
Exclusion screening.
Petition for Limited Access Pursuant to Rule 791.
Proposed order, if useful.
Rule 791 requires filing in the judicial district where charges were disposed and provides that the petition is decided by a court of common pleas judge. ("https://www.pacodeandbulletin.gov/secure/pacode/data/234/chapter7/s791.html")
ARD expungement packet
Collect:
ARD admission order.
Completion proof.
Dismissal order.
Charges.
Victim age if any sexual offense.
Commonwealth objection status.
Order under Rule 320.
Pardon-based packet
Collect:
Pardon document.
Whether pardon is unconditional or conditional.
County and docket.
Certified pardon proof.
Expungement petition if automatic process has not resolved the record.
Clean Slate review if conditional pardon.

sourceSections.title: Pennsylvania Quick Reference
sourceSections.text:
Topic Pennsylvania Rule
Main expungement statute 18 Pa.C.S. 9122
Main limited access statute 18 Pa.C.S. 9122.1
Clean Slate automatic sealing 18 Pa.C.S. 9122.2
Exceptions to Clean Slate 18 Pa.C.S. 9122.3
Effects of relief 18 Pa.C.S. 9122.5
Expungement court-case form Petition under Pa.R.Crim.P. 790
Expungement summary form Petition under Pa.R.Crim.P. 490
Limited access form Petition under Pa.R.Crim.P. 791
ARD expungement Pa.R.Crim.P. 320
Summary conviction expungement 5 years free of arrest/prosecution
Clean Slate summary sealing 5 years after judgment + restitution
Petition limited access misdemeanor 7 conviction-free years + restitution/fee
Clean Slate misdemeanor sealing 7 conviction-free years + restitution
Qualifying offense / felony sealing 10 conviction-free years + restitution
Complete acquittal Automatic expungement path; no partial acquittal
Age-based expungement Age 70 + 10 years arrest/prosecution-free after final release
Pardon expungement Unconditional pardon can lead to expungement
Conditional pardon Clean Slate limited access path
Limited access meaning Sealed from public view, not erased
Expungement meaning Stronger removal/destruction, with limited statutory retained data
Key documents PSP report, docket, OTN, disposition, payment/restitution proof
